prompt.name: setup
prompt.content:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

transcript.text: I’m starting with the codebase scan the setup depends on: app entry points, providers, global styles, aliases, and the Storybook story globs. Once that’s clear, I’ll wire `.storybook/preview.ts` before touching any story files so we can verify each story cleanly one by one.

I’m gathering the project shape now so the preview decorators match the real app instead of guessing. Next I’m checking the entry points, Storybook config, and any provider/theme/router setup the app already uses.

1. Define Your Niche:
Before diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.

2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:
Your Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.

Instagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.

Your Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.

6. Plan Your Content Strategy:
Consistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.

7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:
Instagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. 8. Engage with Your Audience:
Interact with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.

9. Collaborate and Network:
Collaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.

10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:
Keep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.

Starting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. transcript.prompt:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

        title: "How to Start an Instagram Blog",
        href: "/blog/how-to-start-an-instagram-blog",
        description: "Starting an Instagram blog is relatively easy and can be done in a few simple steps and some creativity as well",
        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
        datetime: "2023-03-16",
        imageUrl: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1600096194534-95cf5ece04cf?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80",
        readingTime: '5 min',
        author: {
            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
            imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
        },
        blogPost: `In today's digital age, social media has become an integral part of our lives, and Instagram, with its visually captivating platform, stands out as a popular choice for content creators looking to start a blog. Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.

1. Define Your Niche:
Before diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.

2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:
Your Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.

3. Develop a Cohesive Aesthetic:
Instagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.

4. Craft an Engaging Bio:
Your Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.

5. Share High-Quality Content:
Quality is paramount on Instagram. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing. Additionally, use engaging captions that provide context, ask questions, or encourage interaction.

6. Plan Your Content Strategy:
Consistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.

7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:
Instagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. Use Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and announcements. IGTV allows for longer-form content, giving you an opportunity to showcase in-depth tutorials, travel vlogs, or interviews.

8. Engage with Your Audience:
Interact with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.

9. Collaborate and Network:
Collaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.

10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:
Keep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.

Starting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. Remember, it's not just about followers and likes; it's about creating meaningful connections and sharing your unique voice with the world. So, take the leap, stay true to yourself, and watch your Instagram blog thrive!`

        blogPost: `
Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!


1. Behind-the-Scenes:

Take your followers behind the scenes and show them the process of creating your products or services. People love to see the human side of your brand and get a glimpse of what goes on behind closed doors.


2. User-Generated Content (UGC):

Encourage your followers to create content featuring your products or services and share it on their own profiles. UGC not only gives your brand credibility but also helps foster a sense of community among your followers.


3. Inspirational Quotes:

Share motivational and inspirational quotes that align with your brand values. Quotes are highly shareable and can resonate with your audience on a personal level.


4. Product or Service Highlights:

Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.


5. Tutorials and How-Tos:

Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.


6. Customer Testimonials:

Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.


7. Polls and Surveys:

Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.


8. Throwback Posts:

Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.


9. Seasonal Content:

Tailor your content to fit holidays, seasons, or special occasions. Seasonal posts show that your brand is current and relevant.


10. Fun Facts and Trivia:

Share interesting and fun facts related to your industry or niche. Fun and quirky content can entertain your audience and keep them coming back for more.


11. Collaborations and Partnerships:

Highlight any collaborations or partnerships you have with other brands or influencers. Collaborative content can introduce your brand to a new audience and increase your reach.


12. Company Culture:

Give your followers a glimpse into your company culture and values. Share team photos, company events, and charitable initiatives.


Remember, the key to a successful Instagram feed is to mix up your content and keep it engaging. Experiment with different types of posts and see what resonates best with your audience. Happy posting!`

        },
        blogPost: `Instagram Creator Studio is a powerful tool that allows creators and businesses to manage their Instagram content and account from a desktop computer. If you want to optimize your Instagram presence and streamline your content creation process, here's everything you need to know about Instagram Creator Studio:


1. Overview of Features:

Instagram Creator Studio offers a range of features to help you manage and analyze your content. You can schedule and publish posts, view post insights, manage comments and direct messages, and track audience demographics.


2. Content Scheduling:

One of the key benefits of Instagram Creator Studio is the ability to schedule your posts in advance. This feature allows you to plan and organize your content calendar, ensuring a consistent posting schedule.


3. Insights and Analytics:

Understanding your audience and post performance is crucial for Instagram success. Instagram Creator Studio provides detailed insights and analytics, allowing you to track the reach, engagement, and impressions of your posts.


4. Post Management:

Easily manage your published posts through Instagram Creator Studio. You can edit captions, add location tags, and even delete or archive posts directly from your desktop.


5. Content Library:

The content library feature enables you to organize your media, making it easier to access and reuse your photos and videos for future posts.


6. Direct Messaging:

Stay connected with your followers by managing your Instagram direct messages through Creator Studio. Respond to messages and engage with your audience efficiently.


7. Monetization Features:

For eligible creators, Instagram Creator Studio provides access to monetization features such as IGTV ads and brand partnerships. This opens up opportunities for generating revenue through your Instagram content.


8. Video Publishing:

Creator Studio also supports video publishing, making it convenient to upload, schedule, and manage your IGTV videos directly from your desktop.


9. Cross-Platform Management:

In addition to Instagram, Creator Studio also allows you to manage content for your Facebook Pages. This integrated platform simplifies content management across both social media platforms.


10. Accessible for Instagram Business and Creator Accounts:

Instagram Creator Studio is available for both business accounts and creator accounts. If you have an Instagram business or creator account, you can access Creator Studio for free.


In conclusion, Instagram Creator Studio is a valuable tool for Instagram content creators and businesses alike. Its range of features and user-friendly interface make it a must-have for anyone looking to enhance their Instagram presence. Take advantage of Instagram Creator Studio's capabilities and elevate your Instagram strategy to new heights!`

        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',

        imageUrl:
            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1539187577537-e54cf54ae2f8?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80',

            name: 'Daniela Metz',
            imageUrl:
                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1487412720507-e7ab37603c6f?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
        },
        blogPost: `Looking to up your Instagram game and boost your engagement? Here are 20 tactful Instagram tips to help you create a compelling and successful presence on the platform:


1. Define Your Audience:

Understand your target audience and create content that resonates with their interests and preferences.


2. Showcase Your Personality:

Let your personality shine through your posts and stories to connect with your followers on a personal level.


3. Use High-Quality Images:

Invest in high-quality photography or use professional editing apps to enhance the visual appeal of your posts.


4. Craft Engaging Captions:

Write captivating captions that complement your images and encourage your followers to interact with your content.


5. Leverage Hashtags:

Use relevant and trending hashtags to expand your reach and attract new followers.


6. Engage with Your Audience:

Respond to comments, direct messages, and mentions to foster a sense of community and build meaningful relationships.


7. Collaborate with Others:

Team up with other creators or brands for collaborations and cross-promotion to reach new audiences.


8. Utilize Instagram Stories:

Take advantage of Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and daily updates.


9. Post Consistently:

Maintain a consistent posting schedule to keep your audience engaged and coming back for more.


10. Analyze Your Insights:

Regularly check your Instagram Insights to understand what content performs best and tailor your strategy accordingly.


11. Showcase User-Generated Content (UGC):

Feature UGC from your followers to foster a sense of community and encourage others to create content related to your brand.


12. Host Giveaways and Contests:

Organize fun giveaways and contests to incentivize engagement and attract new followers.


13. Share Behind-the-Scenes:

Offer a glimpse into your creative process or daily life to make your content more relatable and authentic.


14. Use Instagram Reels and IGTV:

Explore Instagram Reels and IGTV for creating entertaining and longer-form content to diversify your feed.


15. Optimize Your Bio:

Craft a concise and impactful bio that clearly defines your brand and what you offer to your followers.


16. Monitor Your Competitors:

Keep an eye on your competitors' strategies to stay informed about industry trends and stay ahead.


17. Follow Instagram Trends:

Stay updated on the latest Instagram features and trends to keep your content fresh and relevant.


18. Use Instagram Shopping:

Leverage Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and stories for a seamless shopping experience.


19. Track Link Clicks:

Use link tracking tools to monitor the performance of links in your bio or swipe-up links in Stories.


20. Stay Authentic and Transparent:

Be true to yourself and your brand values. Authenticity builds trust and loyalty among your followers.


By implementing these tactful Instagram tips, you can elevate your Instagram presence, foster meaningful connections, and create a vibrant and engaged community of followers. Happy Instagramming!`

The codebase analysis showed the app only needs two global Storybook concerns: the shared stylesheet from [`src/index.jsx`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-54-51Z-744a51ba/project/src/index.jsx) and router context from [`src/App.jsx`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-54-51Z-744a51ba/project/src/App.jsx). There’s no app-wide theme/store/i18n layer, and no custom path aliases in [`vite.config.js`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-54-51Z-744a51ba/project/vite.config.js). The Storybook glob in [`main.ts`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-54-51Z-744a51ba/project/.storybook/main.ts) already matched `src/**/*.stories.*`, so I added the preview decorator in [`preview.tsx`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-54-51Z-744a51ba/project/.storybook/preview.tsx#L1) and stabilized Vite’s Storybook test run in [`vite.config.js`](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T18-54-51Z-744a51ba/project/vite.config.js#L28).
